coredata.dc:title: An enzyme-free fluorescent probe based on carbon dots – MnO2 nanosheets for determination of uric acid
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Amjadi, Mohammad
Hallaj, Tooba
Kouhi, Zahra
coredata.dc:description: A turn-on fluorescent probe based on the effect of MnO2 nanosheets on the fluorescence of sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (S,N-CDs) is reported for detection of uric acid. MnO2 nanosheets have several interesting physicochemical properties such as high extinction coefficient and rich redox chemistry, which make them an excellent sensing platform. The fluorescence of S,N-CDs efficiently turns off in the presence of MnO2 nanosheets due to the inner filter effect as well as other quenching mechanisms. By adding uric acid to the solution of S,N-CDs–MnO2 nanosheets, the fluorescence of S,N-CDs is partially recovered as a result of its reaction with MnO2 nanosheets. Under the optimum conditions, the recovered fluorescence intensity displays a linear relationship with the concentration of uric acid in the range of 2.5–30 μM with a detection limit of 0.8 μM. The method was applied for analysis of biological samples with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MnO2 nanosheets
Carbon dots
Uric acid
Turn-on sensor
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of UA in real samples
Detection of UA in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic Illustration of the S,N-CDs-MnO2 probe for detection of UA.
a. TEM image of S,N-CDs, b. TEM image of MnO2 nanosheets, c. AFM image of MnO2 nanosheets.

UV–vis spectrum of MnO2 nanosheets (blue line) and S,N-CDs (black line). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Fluorescence emission spectra of S,N-CDs excited at wavelengths of 300–400 nm with increments of 20 nm.
Fluorescence emission spectra of a. S,N-CD (100 μL) b. S,N-CDs (100 μL) + MnO2 (3.4 μM) + UA (20 μM) c. S,N-CDs (100 μL) + MnO2 (3.4 μM).

a. Stern-Volmer plots for fluorescence quenching of S,N-CDs by MnO2 nanosheets at different temperatures, b. Cross-section of a right-angle fluorescence cell and the geometrical parameters used for correction of IFE. c. Stern-Volmer plots after correction of IFE by Eq. (1). d. Stern-Volmer plots after correction of IFE by Eq. (2).
a. Absorption spectra of MnO2 nanosheets in the presence of various concentrations of UA; b. comparison of absorption spectra of mixture of MnO2 and UA with theoretical sum.
TEM image of MnO2 nanosheets (in optimum conditions) after addition of UA (30 μM) in BR buffer (pH 4).
Tolerance limits of some potentially interfering species in the determination of 10 μM UA under optimum conditions.

row.entry: Tolerance limit (interfering species to analyte ratio)
K+, Na+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, F−, Cl−, NO3 −, SO4 2−
Glucose, Sucrose, Lactose, Glycine, α- Alanine

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results for determination of UA in human plasma and urine samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: mean of three determinations ± standard deviation.

head.ce:title: An enzyme-free fluorescent probe based on carbon dots – MnO2 nanosheets for determination of uric acid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz 5166616471, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A turn-on fluorescent probe based on the effect of MnO2 nanosheets on the fluorescence of sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (S,N-CDs) is reported for detection of uric acid. MnO2 nanosheets have several interesting physicochemical properties such as high extinction coefficient and rich redox chemistry, which make them an excellent sensing platform. The fluorescence of S,N-CDs efficiently turns off in the presence of MnO2 nanosheets due to the inner filter effect as well as other quenching mechanisms. By adding uric acid to the solution of S,N-CDs–MnO2 nanosheets, the fluorescence of S,N-CDs is partially recovered as a result of its reaction with MnO2 nanosheets. Under the optimum conditions, the recovered fluorescence intensity displays a linear relationship with the concentration of uric acid in the range of 2.5–30 μM with a detection limit of 0.8 μM. The method was applied for analysis of biological samples with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: Uric acid (UA) is an important component of plasma and urine which is produced as the end product of dissociation of purine nucleotide. Modifications in the uric acid concentration is the cause of many diseases, biochemical changes or physiological disorders. High level of UA is a sign of gout, excessed alcohol consumption, diabetes, obesity and heart diseases [1] Therefore, UA is a clinically valuable diagnostic marker and its determination in biological fluids is of great importance. UA concentration for a healthy person is in the range of 0.13–0.46 mM in the plasma and 1.49–4.46 mM in urine [2]. Many analytical methods have been proposed for UA monitoring such as fluorescence [2–5], colorimetric [6,7], chemiluminescence [8,9], electrochemical analysis [10–12] and chromatography [13]. However, most of these methods have inherent drawbacks such as having time-consuming process and requirement for enzymes and skilled technicians which limit their application. Between diverse analytical methods, fluorescence assay is a simple, fast and sensitive method. It is important to develop new materials with simple designs and appropriate properties to achieve fluorescent sensors with better performance [4]. Some fluorescence probes based on quantum dots have been reported for detection of UA [4,14,15]. However most of them uses fluorescence turn-off models which have lower sensitivity and selectivity compared with the fluorescence turn-on methods.

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have received much attention in fluorescence sensing and bioimaging due to their high photostability, low cytotoxicity, chemical stability and excellent biocompatibility [16–20].

MnO2 nanosheets which are a new class of layered two dimensional (2D) nanostructures, have been applied for biosensing and bioimaging because of their specific physicochemical properties such as rich redox chemistry, low toxicity, broad absorption spectrum in UV–vis region, high specific surface area and low cost. Ultrathin single-layer MnO2 nanosheets have a unique structure in which one Mn atomic layer sandwiched by two O atomic layers. An edge sharing MnO6 octahedral crystal lattice forms due to coordination of each Mn to six O atoms. The existence of Mn vacancies makes MnO2 nanosheets negatively charged which provides a colloidal stability as a result of repulsion between layers. The single-layer MnO2 nanosheets display a broad absorption spectrum in UV–vis region, which makes it appropriate to design turn-on fluorescence probes. Such probes have been reported for determination of glutathione [16,18,21–23], ascorbic acid and some other biological compounds [17,24–26] and metal ions [27,28]. For instance, Liu et al. have fabricated a fluorescence sensor for determination of ascorbic acid in food samples by suppression of fluorescence of CDs with MnO2 nanosheets. They have attributed this effect to the inner filter effect (IFE) [17]. Wang et al. have developed a switch-on assay based on CDs–MnO2 nanosheets system for detection of glutathione. They have proposed that fluorescence quenching of CDs by MnO2 nanosheets is due to FRET [29].

To the best of our knowledge, the interaction of UA with MnO2 nanosheets has not yet been reported. Herein, taking advantage of MnO2 nanosheets and sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (S,N-CDs), we have developed a fluorescent probe for sensitive and selective determination of UA in real samples. In the proposed sensing system, single-layer MnO2 nanosheets suppress the fluorescence of S,N-CDs. Then, MnO2 decomposes by addition of UA and as a result, fluorescence intensity of S,N-CDs is recovered. The as-prepared nanosensor shows excellent performance in analysis of biological samples.

L-Cysteine, citric acid, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%), manganese chloride tetrahydrate (MnCl2·4H2O), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and uric acid (UA) were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Tetramethylammonium hydroxide pentahydrate (TMA·OH) was purchased from Acros Chemicals (Geel, Belgium). All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Ultra-pure water obtained from aqua MAX – Ultra 370 water purification system (Young Lin instrument Co., Korea) was used for all experiments.

UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by a Cary-100 spectrophotometer (Varian, Australia) with a 1.0 cm quartz cell. The fluorescence spectra were recorded using a Shimadzu RF-540 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Kyoto, Japan) equipped with a 1.0 cm quartz cell. Slit widths of excitation and emission monochromators were set at 2.0 and 5.0 nm, respectively. TEM images of S,N-CDs and MnO2 nanosheets were obtained using a Leo 906 transmission electron microscope (Zeiss, Oberkochen, Germany). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) image were obtained by using a Nanosurf Mobile S instrument (Switzerland).

S,N-CDs were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method by using citric acid and l-cysteine as carbon and heteroatom sources, respectively [30]. 0.2 g citric acid and 0.1 g l-cysteine were mixed and powdered. The solid mixture was transferred into a Teflon equipped stainless steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 3 h in a muffle furnace. After cooling down to room temperature naturally, the pH of resultant syrup was adjusted to 7 with 1 M NaOH and the volume of solution was reached to 50 mL with ultra-pure water and stored at 4 °C. The working solution was prepared by 25-fold dilution of stock solution with water.

Single-layer MnO2 nanosheets were synthesized by previously reported method with a little modification [18,31]. 20 mL of a mixture containing 3.0% H2O2 and 0.6 M TMA·OH was added to 10 mL aqueous solution of 0.3 M MnCl2·4H2O within 15 s. The mixture was stirred vigorously at room temperature overnight in the open air. The resulting product was collected by centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 10 min and washed with ultra-pure water until a clear supernatant was observed. The prepared MnO2 nanosheets were stored at 4 °C for further use. The working solution was obtained by 100-fold dilution of stock solution with ultra-pure water. In order to obtain homogenous suspension, MnO2 nanosheets were dispersed with the aid of sonication. The concentration of MnO2 nanosheets in water was estimated by recording absorption spectrum and using Beer’s law with the molar extinction coefficient of 9.6 × 103 L mol−1 cm−1 at 370 nm [17,18].

For determination of UA, 125 μL of MnO2 nanosheets (139 μM), 100 μL of S,N-CD solution, 75 μL of 0.12 M Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer (pH 4.0) and different volumes of UA standard or sample solution were added to a 5.0 mL volumetric flask and diluted to the volume with water. After incubation for 5 min at room temperature, its fluorescence intensity was measured at 430 nm under the excitation at 360 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of UA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Human plasma samples were obtained from blood transfusion center (Tabriz, Iran). 1.0 mL of acetonitrile was added to 0.5 mL of plasma in a centrifuge tube to precipitate proteins. The sample was centrifuged for 15 min and its clear supernatant was transferred into a 5.0 mL volumetric flask and diluted to the mark with water. A 1.0 mL aliquot of this solution was analyzed according to the general procedure.
Urine samples were diluted 10-fold with deionized water and 250 μL of the diluted urine was taken for analysis according to the general procedure. No further pretreatment was necessary.

At room temperature, aqueous solution of S,N-CDs exhibit high stability for a long-time without any precipitation. TEM image of these CDs in Fig. 1 a, indicates that they are spherical nanoparticles and nearly monodisperse with size distribution in the range of 10–15 nm. To confirm successful synthesis of MnO2 nanosheets, TEM and AFM images (Fig. 1b and c) were obtained. This images show that MnO2 nanosheets have single-layer structure with thickness of about 1.4 nm. To characterize the optical properties of MnO2 nanosheets and S,N-CDs, absorption and fluorescence spectra of their aqueous solution were recorded. As can be seen in Fig. 2 , UV–vis spectrum of MnO2 nanosheets displays characteristic broad absorption peak in the range of 210–600 nm centered at around 370 nm. The yellow S,N-CDs aqueous solution depicts two peaks at 240 and 350 nm in absorption spectrum. As shown in Fig. 3 , the fluorescence emission of S,N-CDs is excitation-independent with maximum excitation and emission wavelength of 360 and 430 nm respectively. Fluorescence quantum yield of S,N-CDs, calculated using quinine sulfate as a standard, was 82%.

As displayed in Fig. S1 (Electronic Supplementary materials, ESM), MnO2 nanosheets have a broad absorption spectrum in the range of 200–600 nm, and there was a large overlap region between the absorption spectrum of MnO2 nanosheets and the fluorescence emission spectrum of S,N-CDs. Moreover, MnO2 nanosheets possess a large molar extinction coefficient of 9.6 × 103 L mol−1 cm−1. Having these properties, MnO2 nanosheets are usually serve as an efficient fluorescence quencher. We obtained the Stern-Volmer plots for fluorescence quenching of S,N-CDs by MnO2 nanosheets at three different temperatures (Fig. 5 a). As can be seen, these plots are not linear and have significant positive curvature. In the next step, we corrected the fluorescence intensities for inner filter effect (IFE). Several equations have been proposed for IFE correction [32,33]. The most complete correction factor which takes into account both primary and secondary IFE is the following equation [33]:
(1)

A
e m
Δ
y
where Icorr and Iobs are corrected and observed fluorescence intensity, respectively and Aex and Aem are absorbance of solution at excitation and emission wavelengths, respectively. Δx, Δy, x1 and y1 are geometrical parameters which are defined in Fig. 5b (In our case, Δx = 0.2 cm, Δy = 0.1 cm, x1 = 0.4 cm and y1 = 0.45 cm). The first term in the right hand side of Eq. (1) is correction factor for primary IFE and the second term is correction factor for secondary IFE. Lakowicz [34] has proposed a simpler equation which is an approximation of Eq. (1) with x1 = x2 = 0.5 cm:
(2)
I
c o r r
I
o b s
=
10
(
0.5
A
e x
+ 0.5
A
e m
)

We corrected the observed fluorescence with both equations and the results were given in Table S1 (ESM). The Stern-Volmer plots by using corrected fluorescence intensities are shown in Fig. 5c and d. As can be seen, the plots are now linear but their slopes are significantly lower than those of uncorrected plots. Moreover by increasing temperature, slopes of plots slightly increase. The obtained Stern-Volmer constants at 20, 30 and 40 °C are 1.03 × 105 (1.01 × 105), 1.13 × 105 (1.10 × 105) and 1.19 × 105 (1.16 × 105) M−1, respectively (the values in parentheses are those obtained by using fluorescence intensities corrected by Eq. (2)). These results show that, although IFE has a significant contribution in fluorescence suppression by MnO2, dynamic quenching and/or FRET are also involved in this process.

In order to achieve the highest sensitivity, effect of some parameters including pH, time and concentration of fluorophore, quencher and buffer was investigated. The effect of pH on the determination of UA was studied in the range of 3–9 by using 0.12 M BR buffer solution. As can be seen in Fig. S2a (ESM), the fluorescence intensity reaches its maximum value at pH 4.0, so this pH was selected as optimum value. Moreover, the optimum concentration of BR buffer for adjusting pH at this value was found to be 2 mM (Fig. S2b). Two effects may be involved in this pH-dependence behavior. MnO2 nanosheets are unstable in acidic medium which cause a decrease in the response of system at lower pH values. On the other hand, the oxidizing power of MnO2 depends on pH and reduces by increasing pH. This causes a reduction in the response of the system at higher pH values. To understand the kinetic behavior of this probe, fluorescence emission spectra were recorded at different times after addition of UA. According to the results shown in Fig. S2c (ESM), maximum fluorescence recovery was obtained after 5 min incubation. One of the important parameters in this sensing system is the concentration of MnO2 nanosheets. To obtain a good sensitivity to UA, the concentration of single layer MnO2 nanosheets should be high enough to quench the S,N-CD fluorescence but not too high to be in large excess. So, an appropriate concentration of MnO2 nanosheets is essential to attain both purposes. As shown in Fig. S2d (ESM), good sensitivity and appropriate background signal was achieved when the concentration of MnO2 nanosheets was 3.47 μM. Finally, in order to investigate the influence of the amount of S,N-CDs on the fluorescence intensity, its effect was examined in the range of 25–200 μL. As can be seen in Fig. S2e (ESM), maximum fluorescence intensity was achieved at S,N-CD volume of 100 μL.

In the optimized experimental conditions, the recovered fluorescence intensity was proportionally increased with the concentration of UA. The calibration curve (Fig. S3, ESM) was linear in the range of 2.5–30 μM with a detection limit (3s) of 0.8 μM. The regression equation was ΔI = 0.425C + 1.59, R 2 = 0.9963, where ΔI is the enhanced fluorescence intensity and C is concentration of UA in μM. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was 3.1% for five replicate determinations of 7.5 μM UA. Table S2 (ESM) compares the analytical performance of the developed method with those of some previously reported luminescence methods for UA determination. As can be seen, the analytical characteristics of this method are comparable with some other methods.

The selectivity of method for determination of UA was evaluated by measuring the fluorescence response of the probe for 10 μM UA in the presence of some species commonly present in plasma and urine samples. Tolerable concentration ratios for interfering species within a relative error of 5% are summarized in Table 1 . Three major potential interferants in this assay are ascorbic acid, l-cysteine and glutathione, but their normal concentrations in biological samples are much lower than that of UA and would not interfere with its determination in such samples. These results show that the method has suitable selectivity for the determination of UA in real biological samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of UA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The developed method was applied for analysis of human plasma and urine samples. To prove the accuracy of the method, recovery tests were carried out by spiking samples with appropriate amounts of UA standard solution. The obtained results are summarized in Table 2 . Statistical analysis of these results using Student’s t-test indicates that there are no significant differences between added and found values.

In summary, we have demonstrated a simple fluorescent probe for UA detection in biological fluids. It makes use of S,N-CDs as fluorescent indicator and MnO2 nanosheets as fluorescence suppressor and analyte-recognition element. UA reacts more or less selectively with MnO2 and decomposes it, leading to recovery of the fluorescence of S,N-CDs. Compared with previous methods, this probe exhibits some appealing properties such as cost-effectiveness, environmental friendliness, good selectivity and rapid “switch-on” fluorescence response. The designed probe has been successfully used for the analysis of UA in human plasma and urine.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2018.02.002.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A simple and sensitive fluorescence based biosensor for the determination of uric acid using H2O2-sensitive quantum dots/dual enzymes
Fluorescent detection of uric acid in biological samples through the inhibition of cobalt(II) catalyzed Amplex UltraRed
High fluorescence S,N co-doped carbon dots as an ultra-sensitive fluorescent probe for the determination of uric acid
A reagent less fluorescent sol–gel biosensor for uric acid detection in biological fluids
Silver nanoparticles plasmon resonance-based method for the determination of uric acid in human plasma and urine samples
A highly selective and sensitive colorimetric uric acid biosensor based on Cu(II)-catalyzed oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine
Chemiluminescence of graphene quantum dots and its application to the determination of uric acid
A novel chemiluminescence paper microfluidic biosensor based on enzymatic reaction for uric acid determination
An inlaying ultra-thin carbon paste electrode modified with functional single-wall carbon nanotubes for simultaneous determination of three purine derivatives
Development of a sandwich format, amperometric screen-printed uric acid biosensor for urine analysis
A review of enzymatic uric acid biosensors based on amperometric detection
Determination of uric acid in human urine by ion chromatography with conductivity detector
Synthesis of positively charged CdTe quantum dots and detection for uric acid
ZnS quantum dots derived a reagentless uric acid biosensor
A rapid fluorescence switch-on assay for glutathione detection by using carbon dots–MnO2 nanocomposites
Switch-on fluorescent sensing of ascorbic acid in food samples based on carbon quantum dots–MnO2 probe
A sensitive turn-on fluorescent probe for intracellular imaging of glutathione using single-layer MnO2 nanosheet-quenched fluorescent carbon quantum dots
Interaction of glucose-derived carbon quantum dots with silver and gold nanoparticles and its application for the fluorescence detection of 6-thioguanine
Novel turn-on fluorescent detection of alkaline phosphatase based on green synthesized carbon dots and MnO2 nanosheets
Turn-on fluorescence sensor for intracellular imaging of glutathione using g-C3N4 nanosheet–MnO2 sandwich nanocomposite
Deciphering the quenching mechanism of 2D MnO2 nanosheets towards Au nanocluster fluorescence to design effective glutathione biosensors
Intracellular glutathione detection using MnO2 nanosheet-modified upconversion nanoparticles
Single-Layer MnO2 nanosheets suppressed Fluorescence of 7-Hydroxycoumarin: mechanistic study and application for sensitive sensing of ascorbic acid in vivo
Switch-on fluorescence sensor for ascorbic acid detection based on MoS2 quantum dots-MnO2 nanosheets system and its application in fruit samples
A fluorescent glucose bioassay based on the hydrogen peroxide-induced decomposition of a quencher system composed of MnO2 nanosheets and copper nanoclusters
Layered MnO2 nanosheet as a label-free nanoplatform for rapid detection of mercury(II)
Single-layer MnO2 nanosheet quenched fluorescence ruthenium complexes for sensitive detection of ferrous iron
A FRET-based carbon dot–MnO2 nanosheet architecture for glutathione sensing in human whole blood samples
Sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots as the chemiluminescence probe for detection of Cu2+ ions
Room-temperature synthesis of manganese oxide monosheets
Assessment of inner filter effect corrections in fluorimetry
Improvement of inner filter effect correction based on determination of effective geometric parameters using a conventional fluorimeter
